title: Spin-orbit interaction in core-shell semiconductor-metal nanowires
authors: Tudor-Gabriel Dumitru, Anna Sitek, Gunnar Thorgilsson, Sigurdur I. Erlingsson, Andrei Manolescu
abstract: We study theoretically the spin-orbit interaction of electrons confined in a tubular semiconductor nanowire, between an inner semiconductor core and an outer metallic extra shell. A band off-offset potential is present at the inner semiconductor-semiconductor interface and a more complex potential barrier at the outer metal-semiconductor contact. The cross section of the nanowire has a hexagonal geometry. We use a model derived from the k-dot-p method, and discuss the effects of the interface potentials on the strength of the spin-orbit coupling and on the localization of the wave functions within the semiconductor shell
summary: This paper theoretically examines how a metallic shell influences the spin-orbit interaction and electron distribution in hexagonal semiconductor nanowires. It finds that while the metallic contact does not significantly boost spin-orbit coupling, it plays a crucial role in narrowing the electron distribution and affecting state localization.
main_problem: Investigating how the spin-orbit interaction (SOI) and electron wave function localization in a core-shell semiconductor nanowire are affected by the presence of an external metallic shell and its associated potential profile.
main_result: The metallic contact's contribution to increasing SOI strength is minimal; instead, the primary effect of the metallic potential is to enhance the spatial localization of electrons and increase the energy separation between corner-localized and side-localized states.
method: The study uses a k.p method-derived Hamiltonian and a folding-down procedure, with the metallic contact potential derived from Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the SIESTA package.
model_or_system: A tubular, hexagonal-geometry nanowire consisting of an InP core, an InAs semiconductor shell, and an outer metallic (Al) shell, featuring a band-offset potential and a Z-shaped potential barrier at the metal-semiconductor interface.
key_observables: Spin-orbit interaction strength, energy dispersion (energy levels vs. kz), and spatial localization of wave functions.
important_parameters: Shell thickness (d), core radius (R), potential penetration lengths (r and s), and potential magnitudes (well depth and barrier height).
main_assumptions: The model assumes an infinite wire Hamiltonian and uses a simplified Z-shaped potential profile for the metallic contact.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the spatial profile of the confining potential; Figure 2 displays energy levels for different shell thicknesses without SOI; Figure 3 and 4 illustrate the energy dispersion and the Rashba SOI effect under various metallic potential regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and potential landscape, describes the k.p Hamiltonian and DFT-derived potential, presents numerical results regarding energy dispersion and degeneracy lifting, and concludes with an analysis of wave function localization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Spatial profile of the confining potential across the nanowire. (a) 1D profile along the x-axis (corner to corner) detailing the potential magnitude. (b) The cross-sectional coverage of the potentials. This configuration assumes a shell thickness d = 20 nm. The band-offset decay length r (gray region) and the external penetration depth s (red region) are both set to 10 nm.
Figure 2: Energies of the first 24 states for a shell thickness of d = 10 nm (a) and d = 20 nm (b), using Vwire = 0.

Figure 3: Spin-orbit effect on the energy dispersion of the first 24 states for a shell thickness d = 20 nm and penetration length r = 10 nm. Panel (a) shows the case of zero external penetration (s = 0) with the effects being attributed entirely to the extended band-offset potential, while (b) and (c) show the effects for a penetration length of s = 10 nm. Panel (b) uses a well depth of −50 meV and a barrier height of 30 meV, whereas (c) uses −150 meV for the depth of the well and a 90 meV barrier.

Figure 4: Spin-orbit effect on the energy dispersion of the first 24 states for a shell thickness d = 20 nm and penetration length d = r = 20 nm. Panel (a) shows the case of zero external penetration (s = 0) with the effects being attributed entirely to the extended band-offset potential, while (b) and (c) show the effects for a penetration length of s = 10 nm. Panel (b) uses a well depth of −50 meV and a barrier height of 30 meV, whereas (c) uses −150 meV for the depth of the well and a 90 meV barrier.
